english: pay wages; to bribe, to corrupt. -- 2. in C. at present a kind of sacrifice. II. vb. to reward, to recompense, perh. better brṅán-pa (brngan pa).

1. (cf. rṅám-pa (rngam pa)) to desire earnestly, to crave, bkrés-rṅab-pa (bkres rngab pa) to be greedy, to have a craving appetite Lex. -- 2. W. col. for rṅá-ba (rnga ba) to mow; *ṅáb-sa* (ngab sa) (lit. ṅáb-rtsva (ngab rtsva)) W. grass or corn that is to be mown or cut.
Med., a hollow horn, used for sucking Sch.

1. sbst. (cf. dṅom (dngom)), also rṅam-brjid (rngam brjid), rṅom-brjid (rngom brjid), splendour, magnificence, majesty, an appearance, commanding awe or inspiring terror (but not = awe Cs.); rṅám-pai ṅá-ro (rngam pai nga ro) a voice of that kind; rṅám-po (rngam po), rṅám-ċan (rngam can) adj. bright, shining, grand, majestic. -- 2. vb., also rṅáms-pa (rngams pa), pf. brṅams (brngams), to breathe, rṅám-pa bde (rngam pa bde) the breathing is regular Miṅ., frq; rṅám-pa tuṅ (rngam pa tung) short breath Sch.; esp. to breathe heavily, to pant, rṅám-pa rgod (rngam pa rgod) wild puffing Med.; c. dat. to pant for, to desire ardently, srog gċód-pa-la (srog gcod pa la) to be blood-thirsty Ma.; rṅám-pai tsúl-gyis (rngam pai tshul gyis) greedily (devouring) Thgr.; rṅám-ċan (rngam can) adj. greedy, avaricious, covetous; *za-ṅam-pa* (za ngam pa) voracious, gluttonous, ravenous W.; to rush upon, fly at, throw one's self on, gźán-la (gzhan la) on others Mil.; to rage, to be in a fury; to destroy or murder in a state of fury; mi pál-ċér grir rṅom (mi phal cher grir rngom) (like grir gsod (grir gsod)) the people are in numbers murdered by the sword Ma.; to call out in a rage, ċes krós-rṅam-nas (ces khros rngam nas) thus she called furious with rage Dzl.; rṅáms-pai (kro)-źal (rngams pai (khro)-zhal) an angry face, wrathful look Glr.
height; in height Glr., rṅáms-su (rngams su) id.

pf. (b)rṅubs ((b)rngubs), fut. brṅub (brngub), imp. rṅubs (rngubs), to draw in, dbugs (dbugs) air, sñar (snar) into the nose Med.; to breathe Med.; dbugs rṅub mi _ton (dbugs rngub mi thon) (?) is mentioned as a sign of great sadness and affliction Pth.

perspiration, sweat, rṅúl-ċu (rngul cu) id., esp. col.; rṅul _du (rngul du) Med., _byuṅ (byung) Dzl., rṅúl-ċu _ton (rngul cu thon), *yoṅ* (yong), col. perspiration is breaking forth; rṅul _dóṅ-pa (rngul dong pa) to cause to sweat or perspire Cs.; rṅúl-ba (rngul ba), pf. brṅul (brngul), to sweat, to perspire Cs.
1. also rṅeu-ċúṅ (rnge'u cung), rṅa-ċúṅ (rnga cung), a little drum, diminutive of rṅa (rnga). -- 2. the young of a camel, v. rṅa-móṅ (rnga mong).

is stated to be a kind of leprosy, covering the whole body, of a whitish colour, itching very much, and contagious; *ṅo p'og, gyab* (ngo phog, gyab) he is affected with leprosy; rṅó-ċan (rngo can) leprous (cf. mdze (mdze)).

to be able Cs., rṅo-tóg-pa (rngo tog pa) id., so Fouc. Gyatch. 220, 9, *ṅób-ċe* (ngob ce) Ld.; Sch. has: rṅo mi tóg-pa (rngo mi tog pa) to look at one with uncertainty, not being able to recognize; the passage of Mil.: rṅo ma togs kyaṅ (rngo ma togs kyang) is not to be explained by either of these significations; Lex.: rṅo mi togs (rngo mi togs) w.e.
to roast, fry, (?) Sch. v. rṅód-pa (rngod pa).

1. also ze-rṅóg (ze rngog), the hunch or hump of an animal Lex., more esp. a hump consisting of fat (like that of the camel); tsil-rṅóg (tshil rngog) the fat around the kidneys, suet Mil.; rús-kyi rṅog (rus kyi rngog) Lex. w.e. -- 2. rṅog(-ma) (rngog (-ma)) the mane of horses etc. (not of the lion, v. ral-pa (ral pa)), rta-rṅóg (rta rngog) a horse's mane, dre-rṅóg (dre rngog) a mule's mane; dreu-rṅóg (dre'u rngog) a kind of stuffed seat or mattress Cs., a thick-haired carpet Sch.; rṅóg-ċan (rngog can), rṅóg-ldan (rngog ldan) having a mane; rṅogs-ċágs (rngogs cags) a beast that has a mane Cs.

I. pf. brṅos (brngos), fut. brṅod (brngod) Cs. (perh. erron. for brṅo (brngo)) imp. rṅod (rngod), rṅos (rngos), W. *ṅo-ċe* (ngo ce) 1. to parch (barley), ser tsam (ser tsam) (to parch a thing) so that it turns yellowish Glr. -- 2. to roast, to fry e.g. meat in a pan. II. to deceive (acc. to Cs. = rṅón-pa (rngon pa) to deceive wild beasts, to hunt); to seduce, esp. to sensual indulgence, bud-méd (bud med) Lex.; similarly Tar. 39, 2.
